name: Science Fiction
description: Stories exploring speculative technology, future societies, or alternative realities, using 'what if' premises to test ideas and consequences.

nodes.label: Science Fiction Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story that explores a speculative premise — 'what if X were true?' — where the consequences of that premise are rigorously followed through society, technology, and human behavior.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as science fiction
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
failure_modes: Speculative premise is window dressing with no consequences explored
Technology is magic with a science label
signals_in_text: Early introduction of the speculative premise
World visibly differs from ours in specific, logical ways

nodes.label: Story Must Be Driven by a Speculative Premise
nodes.definition: The story must center on a 'what if' — a speculative technology, scientific discovery, social change, or alternative reality — and that premise must drive the plot rather than merely decorate it.
exit_conditions: The speculative premise is essential — remove it and the story collapses
failure_modes: Premise could be removed without affecting the story
Premise is mentioned but its implications are not explored
signals_in_text: Characters grapple with the premise's implications
Plot events are consequences of the premise
Conflict arises from the premise itself

nodes.label: Speculative Elements Must Be Internally Consistent
nodes.definition: The speculative elements must follow their own rules consistently — if FTL travel exists, it always works the same way; if AI is sentient, it behaves consistently with its established nature.
exit_conditions: Rules of the speculative element are respected throughout
typical_variants: Consistent within genre conventions (space opera physics)
failure_modes: Technology capabilities change based on plot needs
Rules established early are broken later for convenience
signals_in_text: Characters understand and reference technological limitations
Audience can predict what technology will and won't do

nodes.label: Consequences Must Be Explored
nodes.definition: The story must show how the speculative premise affects individuals, society, ethics, or the human condition — not just that the technology exists, but what it means for people.
exit_conditions: The premise's effects on human life, society, or ethics are visible
typical_variants: Personal consequences (how it changes one life)
Existential consequences (what it reveals about humanity)
failure_modes: Technology exists but no one is affected by it
Consequences are superficial or ignored
signals_in_text: Characters debate the implications
Society has adapted (or failed to adapt) to the premise
Moral questions arise directly from the speculative element

nodes.label: Speculative Premise Must Feel Plausible
nodes.definition: The premise must feel like it could happen — even if it requires extrapolation — given an internal logic the audience can follow. 'Could' doesn't mean 'scientifically likely' but 'logically coherent.'
exit_conditions: Audience accepts the premise as plausible within the story's framework
failure_modes: Premise feels arbitrary or magical
No attempt to justify or ground the speculation
signals_in_text: Technical or social details that ground the premise
Characters take the premise seriously
Worldbuilding supports the plausibility

nodes.label: Hard Science Fiction
nodes.definition: SF where scientific accuracy and technical rigor are central — the speculative premise is grounded in real or extrapolated science, and problem-solving through scientific reasoning drives the plot.
exit_conditions: Technology and science are explained, consistent, and central to problem-solving
failure_modes: Claims scientific rigor but physics are wrong
Science is accurate but story is a textbook not a narrative
signals_in_text: Technical detail in descriptions
Characters solve problems through scientific reasoning
Real physics and engineering constrain the plot

nodes.label: Social Science Fiction
nodes.definition: SF focused on how speculative changes affect society, politics, culture, and human relationships — the 'what if' is primarily social rather than technological.
entry_conditions: Social implications are the primary focus
exit_conditions: Story explores how a speculative change reshapes human society and relationships
failure_modes: Social commentary overwhelms narrative
Society described but not dramatized through characters
signals_in_text: Characters navigating altered social norms
Political and cultural institutions shaped by the premise
Interpersonal relationships affected by the speculative change

nodes.label: Space Opera
nodes.definition: SF that emphasizes adventure, drama, and grand scale — interstellar civilizations, galactic conflicts, and epic scope — with looser scientific constraints but strong narrative momentum.
entry_conditions: Setting is interstellar or galactic in scope
exit_conditions: Story delivers adventure and drama at civilizational scale
failure_modes: Scale is vast but stakes are impersonal
Adventure without any speculative substance
signals_in_text: Multiple star systems or civilizations
Fleet battles or diplomatic crises
Individual characters against civilizational backdrop

nodes.label: Dystopian / Post-Apocalyptic
nodes.definition: SF set in a degraded or collapsed future — exploring what happens when speculative premises go wrong, societies fail, or catastrophic events reshape human existence.
entry_conditions: Setting is a degraded or collapsed future
exit_conditions: Story explores survival, resistance, or adaptation in a broken world
failure_modes: Dystopia is aesthetic — oppression has no mechanisms
Post-apocalypse is adventure playground with no weight
signals_in_text: Detailed mechanisms of social control or collapse
Characters constrained by systemic oppression or scarcity
Memory of what was lost

nodes.label: Technology Has Defined Capabilities and Limits
nodes.definition: The story's speculative technology must have explicitly defined capabilities and limitations that the audience can track — creating opportunities for clever solutions and meaningful constraints.
exit_conditions: Audience understands what the technology can and cannot do
failure_modes: Technology does whatever the plot needs
Limits are stated but routinely violated
signals_in_text: Characters reference limitations
Technology fails in predictable ways
Solutions work within established constraints

nodes.label: Society Has Adapted to the Speculative Premise
nodes.definition: The story's society must reflect the speculative premise — institutions, economies, cultures, and daily life must show the visible effects of the technology or change that defines this world.
exit_conditions: Society is visibly different from ours in ways that follow from the premise
failure_modes: Present-day society with future technology bolted on
Society mentioned but not shown
signals_in_text: Background details reflect the premise
Characters' occupations, relationships, and routines differ from ours
Institutions have adapted to the speculative reality

nodes.label: Recognizable Human Core Despite Extrapolation
nodes.definition: However far the speculative premise extrapolates, the story must maintain a recognizable human emotional and psychological core — the audience must be able to connect with characters despite the alien context.
exit_conditions: Characters feel emotionally real despite their speculative context
typical_variants: Universal human dilemmas in speculative settings
failure_modes: Characters are so alien they're unrelatable
Emotional life is subordinated to technical worldbuilding
signals_in_text: Characters express recognizable desires, fears, and attachments
Interpersonal dynamics feel real despite speculative setting

nodes.label: Premise Establishment / World Difference Scene
nodes.definition: An early scene that establishes the speculative premise and shows the audience how this world differs from ours — the 'what if' made concrete.
exit_conditions: Audience understands the speculative premise and its visible effects
typical_variants: Contrast between old and new world
failure_modes: Premise explained through exposition dump
World difference is trivial or cosmetic
signals_in_text: Show-don't-tell demonstration of the premise
Character interaction with speculative elements
Audience immediately sees how this world is different

nodes.label: Consequence / Ethical Dilemma Scene
nodes.definition: A scene where the consequences of the speculative premise create a moral dilemma, social conflict, or human cost that couldn't exist in our world — the premise's implications made personal.
entry_conditions: Premise established and characters are living within it
exit_conditions: Character faces a choice or consequence that is unique to this speculative world
typical_variants: Social conflict arising from speculative change
Personal cost of living in this world
Discovery that the premise has hidden consequences
failure_modes: Dilemma could exist in any setting — premise is irrelevant
Consequence is theoretical rather than dramatized
signals_in_text: Character must make a choice that requires the speculative context
Emotional weight comes directly from the premise
Audience thinks about what they would do in this situation

nodes.label: Premise-Dependent Resolution Scene
nodes.definition: The climactic resolution must depend on the speculative premise — the solution, sacrifice, or transformation must be one that could only happen in this speculative world.
entry_conditions: All escalation has led to this point
exit_conditions: Central conflict is resolved in a way that requires the speculative premise
typical_variants: Social revolution enabled by the premise
Sacrifice unique to this world's rules
Transcendence or transformation through the premise
failure_modes: Resolution is generic and doesn't use the premise
Solution violates established speculative rules
signals_in_text: Resolution uses established rules creatively
Outcome could not happen in our world
Premise's full implications are realized

nodes.label: Intellectual Engagement and Curiosity
nodes.definition: SF requires a tone that engages the audience's intellect — curiosity about how things work, what could happen, and what it would mean. The tone invites thinking, not just feeling.
exit_conditions: Story stimulates curiosity and thought alongside emotional engagement
failure_modes: Story is purely emotional with no intellectual dimension
Intellectual content is dry and disconnected from narrative
signals_in_text: Characters think about implications
Audience is invited to consider the 'what if'
Technical or social details reward attention

nodes.label: Technology as Magic
nodes.definition: SF fails when technology operates as magic — with no consistent rules, no understandable limits, and no grounding in any extrapolative logic. 'Sufficiently advanced technology' is not an excuse for arbitrary capabilities.
entry_conditions: Technology is used in the story
exit_conditions: Technology operates within defined, consistent rules
typical_variants: Technology does whatever the plot requires
Scientific terminology used to disguise magical thinking
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Technology capabilities surprise the audience without setup
No consistent rules govern what technology can do

edges.label: Promise requires a speculative premise
edges.effects_on_stakes: The premise defines what kind of conflict is possible
edges.effects_on_character: Characters exist in a world shaped by the premise
common_alternatives: Single major premise vs. multiple interconnected premises
anti_patterns: No clear speculative premise — just set in the future

edges.label: Promise requires internal consistency
edges.effects_on_character: Characters can make informed decisions based on known rules
common_alternatives: Strict scientific consistency vs. consistent genre conventions
anti_patterns: Rules change based on plot needs

edges.label: Promise requires explored consequences
edges.effects_on_stakes: Consequences create the story's conflict and meaning
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are affected by and respond to the premise's implications
common_alternatives: Personal vs. societal vs. ethical consequences
anti_patterns: Premise exists but its effects on people are never shown

edges.label: Promise requires plausible speculation
edges.effects_on_character: Characters inhabit a world the audience can believe in
anti_patterns: Premise is arbitrary with no attempt at grounding

edges.label: Promise requires intellectual engagement
edges.effects_on_character: Characters think as well as feel
anti_patterns: No intellectual dimension — speculative setting is purely decorative

edges.label: Premise explored with scientific rigor
preconditions: Premise is grounded in real or extrapolated science
edges.effects_on_character: Characters solve problems through scientific reasoning
anti_patterns: Claims scientific rigor but physics are wrong

edges.label: Premise explored through social impact
preconditions: Premise primarily affects society and human relationships
edges.effects_on_stakes: Social disruption and adaptation define the conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters navigate a society reshaped by the premise

edges.label: Premise at galactic scale with adventure focus
preconditions: Premise enables interstellar civilization and conflict

edges.label: Premise gone wrong — degraded future
preconditions: Premise has led to societal collapse or oppression
edges.effects_on_stakes: Survival and resistance define the conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Characters live under constraint and seek freedom or meaning
anti_patterns: Dystopian aesthetic without mechanisms of oppression

edges.label: Hard SF requires rigorous technology rules
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must understand and work within technical limits
anti_patterns: Technology has no defined limits despite claims of scientific rigor

edges.label: Social SF requires visibly adapted society
edges.effects_on_stakes: Social structures create constraints and conflicts
edges.effects_on_character: Characters navigate adapted institutions and norms
anti_patterns: Modern society with one speculative element and no adaptation

edges.label: Space opera requires human anchor despite grand scale
edges.effects_on_stakes: Grand stakes are felt through individual human experience
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are relatable despite extraordinary context
common_alternatives: Personal love story against galactic war
Individual moral choice with civilizational consequences
anti_patterns: Characters are as alien as their setting — no emotional connection

edges.label: Dystopia requires visible social mechanisms
edges.effects_on_stakes: Oppressive mechanisms create the constraints characters face
edges.effects_on_character: Characters live under visible, enforceable social control
anti_patterns: Dystopia is aesthetic — no mechanisms of control shown

edges.label: Technology rules generate premise establishment scene
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience learns the rules that will govern the conflict
edges.effects_on_character: Character demonstrates or encounters the technology
anti_patterns: Technology explained through exposition dump rather than scene

edges.label: Adapted society creates ethical dilemma scenes
edges.effects_on_stakes: Social structure creates dilemmas unique to this world
edges.effects_on_character: Character faces moral choices that require the speculative context
anti_patterns: Dilemma is generic and could exist in any setting

edges.label: Human anchor makes consequences personal
preconditions: Human emotional core established in speculative context
edges.effects_on_stakes: Consequences are felt emotionally, not just intellectually
edges.effects_on_character: Speculative consequences become personal and relatable

edges.label: Consequences build to premise-dependent resolution
preconditions: Consequences of the premise have been dramatized
edges.effects_on_stakes: Resolution addresses the premise's central question
edges.effects_on_character: Character's final action is meaningful because of the speculative context

edges.label: Inconsistent technology becomes magic
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience stops trusting the world's logic
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
